coredata.dc:title: Determination of thiomersal and mercurial residues by photo-degradation and flow injection analysis with luminescence probing using carbon quantum dots prepared from thiourea

coredata.dc:creator: Franco, Claudiomar R.
Miranda-Andrades, Jarol R.
Toloza, Carlos A.T.
Larrudé, Dunieskys G.
Maqueira-Espinosa, Luis
Aucelio, Ricardo Q.
De Falco, Anna
Pedrozo-Peñafiel, Marlin J.
coredata.dc:description: Thiomersal is an organomercurial compound but, despite that, it is widely used in vaccines formulations to inactivate organisms and toxins and to prevent microbial growth during storage and use. In this work, thiomersal (along with traces of mercurial residues) was determined after photo-degradation (using UV and H2O2) to produce Hg2+ that was determined by flow injection analysis using luminescence probing with carbon quantum dots (prepared from citric acid and thiourea). Linear analytical response was observed in the concentration range of 25 to 900 µg L−1 and the method was efficiently applied to the determination of thiomersal in vaccines against hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus with recoveries ranging from 93 to 94%.

Thiomersal
Vaccines
Flow injection analysis
Photo-degradation
Carbon quantum dots

Comparative results using an analytical method based on photo-degradation CV-AAS
Preliminary studies of the interaction between Hg2+ and carbon quantum dots
Application of the method to flow injection analysis
Analytical figures of merit in FIA
Study of possible interferences in determining thiomersal in vaccines
Determination of thiomersal using the CQDs-Tu probe in flow regime after off-line photo-degradation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially financed by Coordenacao de Aperfeicoamento de Pessoal de Nivel Superior (CAPES-Brazil - Finance Code 001). Aucelio thanks scholarships from Brazilian agencies FAPERJ (E-26/200.810/2021) and CNPq (306108/2021-6) and scientific grants from FAPERJ (SEI 260003/001187/2020 Ref 210.104/2020 and SEI 260003/01131/2021 Ref 210.726/2021) and CNPq (409090/2018-2). Pedrozo-Peñafiel thanks FAPERJ (E-26/202.408/2021) for the scholarships. Miranda-Andrades thanks FAPERJ (E-201.951/2020–SEI-26003/002294). Authors are also thankful for analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio).

Authors are also thankful for analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intensity of the quenched photoluminescence produced by Hg2+ (at 50 µg L−1) over time: (a) GQDs-GSH (b) GQDs (c) GQDs-TA (d) GQDs-TU.
(A) FE-STEM images with size histogram of average diameter of CQDs-TU. Histograms for the: (B) Aspect ratio and (C) Circularity.

(A) Influence of pH on measured photoluminescence from CQDs-TU dispersion and (B) Spectral profiles at different pH values from a to h (a: pH 2; b: pH 3; c: pH 4; d: pH 5; e: pH 6; f: pH 7; g: pH 8; h: pH 9). (C) ξ potentials of CQDs-TU in function of the pH.

Normalized curves for CQDs-TU in aqueous dispersions in function of the concentration of Hg2+ (sensitivity values in parenthesis): (a) 20 °C (3.6 × 10−2 L μg−1) (b) 25 °C (2.5 × 10−2 L μg−1) (c) 30 °C (1.8 × 10−2 L μg−1) (d) 35 °C (1.0 × 10−2 L μg−1). Each point is an average of 3 measurements with standard deviation less than 2%.

(A) Fiagram showing peaks due to variation of CQDs-TU photoluminescence in the presence of Hg2+. Standard solutions of Hg2+ at (a) 0 (b) 25 (c) 100 (d) 150 (e) 300 (f) 450 (g) 600 (h) 750 (i) 900 µg L−1. (B) Normalized analytical curve for the CQDs-TU photoluminescence quenching in presence of Hg2+ using FIA: (L0/L) = (1.32 ± 0.02) × 10−3 CHg2+ (L μg−1) + (1.10 ± 0.004) (R2 = 0.997).
Pictogram report of the AGREE metric for the proposed method.
Optimized operating parameters employed in FIA system.

head.ce:title: Determination of thiomersal and mercurial residues by photo-degradation and flow injection analysis with luminescence probing using carbon quantum dots prepared from thiourea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro- RJ, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aChemistry Department, Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro- RJ, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Natural and Exact Sciences, Universidad de la Costa, Barranquilla, Colombia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Natural and Exact Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Natural and Exact Sciences, Universidad de la Costa, Barranquilla, Colombia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering School, Mackenzie Presbyterian University, São Paulo, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cEngineering School, Mackenzie Presbyterian University, São Paulo, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiomersal is an organomercurial compound but, despite that, it is widely used in vaccines formulations to inactivate organisms and toxins and to prevent microbial growth during storage and use. In this work, thiomersal (along with traces of mercurial residues) was determined after photo-degradation (using UV and H2O2) to produce Hg2+ that was determined by flow injection analysis using luminescence probing with carbon quantum dots (prepared from citric acid and thiourea). Linear analytical response was observed in the concentration range of 25 to 900 µg L−1 and the method was efficiently applied to the determination of thiomersal in vaccines against hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus with recoveries ranging from 93 to 94%.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal is an organomercurial compound used in vaccine production to inactivate microorganisms and toxins and to keep a sterile production line [1]. It also helps preventing microbial growth during vaccines storage and handling [1]. Thiomersal degradation produces ethylmercury in addition to thiosalicylic acid, and dithiobenzoic acid [2]. There is some degree of concern over the safety of using thiomersal in vaccines due to the toxicity of ethylmercury and bioaccumulation of mercury, which has been linked to neurological disorders [3]. Therefore, the concentration of thiomersal in vaccines must be restricted to safe limits in order to minimize negative effects on humans. The routine monitoring of vaccines, related to the degradation of thiomersal, requires easy to use and high throughput analytical methods that are sensitive enough to determine total mercury in these samples. Besides, it is highly desirable that developed methods complies with principles of green analytical chemistry.

The interaction of CdTe quantum dots with thimerosal was studied in order to propose a method for indirect quantification of thiomersal in vaccines by measuring photoluminescence quenching. Under optimized conditions, limit of detection (LOD) was 26.6 μg L−1 [4]. Square-wave voltammetry was used for the determination of thiomersal in vaccines using a La2O3 decorated carbon-paste modified electrode, leading to LOD of 36 μg L−1 [5]. A method for the determination of thimerosal in vaccines, based on decomposition organic mercury compounds into inorganic mercury, was proposed using a mixture of oxidizing agents (KBr/KBrO3 and KMnO4) and cold vapor (CV) atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) with LOD of 0.02 µg L−1 [6]. Flow injection analysis (FIA) was used for the determination of thiomersal based on-line UV and microwave (MW) treatment to oxidize organic mercury to Hg2+, followed by quantification of Hg0 by CV-AFS. The LOD was 0.003 μg mL−1 and the method was used to analyze ophthalmic solutions [7].

Sandoval et al. provided the photocatalytic degradation of thiomersal using visible radiation and a ZnFe2O4/poly(o-phenylenediamine) composite as photocatalyst. Thiomersal degradation by-products and the generated Hg0 were respectively detected by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and AFS [8]. The photochemical decomposition of thiomersal to Hg2+ and subsequent exposure to a drop of Au nanorods dispersion, to in situ generate Hg0, allowed its determination by colorimetry. The spectrophotometric analysis of the Hg-enriched drop lead to LOD of 0.5 ng mL−1 (result reported as Hg) in pharmaceutical samples [9]. Photo-degradation of thiomersal using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was reported by Miranda-Andrades et al., producing ethylmercury and then Hg2+. Detection was made after chemical reduction of Hg2+ and detection using atomic absorption spectrometry (LOD of 20 ng L−1) [10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) comprises a myriad of nanometric carbon structures where electronic transport is confined within their dimensions. Because of that, they exhibit peculiar optical and catalytic properties, due to quantum confinement. One example is the inherent photoluminescence (due to excitonic recombination) that occurs in the visible range of the spectrum. CQDs are bio-compatible and present low toxicity, therefore, they are becoming an attractive alternative nanomaterial especially for biomedical and environmental applications [11,12]. Due to their nanometric size, CQDs are stabilized in aqueous solutions, forming a nanoparticle dispersion with a very large surface area to interact with chemical species in solution, producing changes in its intrinsic photoluminescence (intensity variation, spectral shifting and changing in lifetime) that often is related to the concentration of the interacting species. Therefore, a quantitative model can be established with CQDs dispersion being used as analytical photoluminescent sensor.

Flow injection analysis (FIA) systems is an excellent way to perform automated analysis due to the easiness of operation, potential for miniaturization, low reagent and sample consumption, low generation of waste, high analytical frequency and versatility in performing reactions (or derivatizations) with possibility to quantify products before reaching chemical equilibrium. In this work, the indirect quantification of thiomersal was proposed after UV photochemical degradation that produces ethylmercury and then Hg2+. The resultant sample solution was probed using photoluminescent carbon quantum dots, prepared from citric acid an tiourea, leading to the quantification of Hg2+ using a FIA system adapted to a spectrofluorimeter. In this way, quantification of total mercury content was made in hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus vaccines.

Photoluminescence measurements (spectral analysis and steady state measurements with and without coupling to the FIA system) were employed on a FluoroMax®-4 spectrofluorimeter (Horiba, Japan). A FIALab 2000 system (FIAlab Instruments, USA) was employed for FIA. Comparative mercury measurements were made by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) using a mercury cold vapor dedicated system, model RA-915 (LUMEX, Russia), connected to an RP-92 accessory for chemical reduction of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. A field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, model JSM-6701F, Japan), operating in the transmission mode (at 30 kV, with a 6 mm work distance and using a secondary electron image detector), was used to obtain images of the GQDs. Zeta potential measurements were made on Zetasizer Nano ZS with MPT-2 titrator (Malvern, UK). Total carbon measurements were made on a Carbon Analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Japan). The pHmeter was a model mPA 210 from Tecnopon (Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode conjugated with an Ag|AgCl(KClsat) reference electrode.

The laboratory-made photochemical reactor consisted of six commercial fluorescent lamps (6 W each, emitting in the visible spectral range; 380–750 nm) fixed inside of a PVC tube (20 cm diameter × 30 cm width). The PVC tube was mounted into a wood box with a mechanical rotor, placed at the base, to move a test-tube circular rotating rack (6 rotations min−1) localized inside the PVC tube. Samples were placed into 20 mL quartz tubes, which were placed (vertically) inside the reactor in a circular test-tube rack.

Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from the Milli-Q gradient A10 ultra-purifier (Millipore, USA). Thiomersal, thiosalicylic acid, 2,2′dithiodibenzoic acid, citric acid, glutathione (GSH), thiourea (TU), thioacetamide (TA), hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Mercury chloride (at 1000 µg L− 1) was from Brooks Rand Instruments (USA). Tin chloride (SnCl2) and dimethyl sulfoxide were from Vetec (Brazil). Dialysis membranes (able to retain molecular weight of 3.5 kDa) were from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (USA).

CQDs were produced using an eco-friendly method (without high temperatures or high pressure systems) based on procedures reported in literature [13,14] using the pyrolysis and hydro-exfoliation of an organic precursor. For this work, it was used mixtures of citric acid (1.0 g) and 0.3 g of a nitrogen/sulfur containing chemical modifier: thiourea (TU), glutathione (CSH) or thioacetamide (TA). CQDs were also prepared using only citric acid in order to evaluate the effect in using the chemical modifier. Solid organic precursor(s) were mixed and placed in a beaker, on a heating plate, to be heated until the melted material appeared light brown in color. This obtained mass was poured into a beaker containing 50.0 mL of water, at room temperature, forming a light yellow mixture, containing CQDs. The mixture was then dialyzed for 24 h in using a dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa).

The parameters employed in flow analysis are presented in Table 1 . The pump and injection valve were controlled by FIALab for Windows 5.0 software (FIALab Instruments) and the spectrofluorimeter was used as the detector. The operating parameters such as flow rates, sample introduction volume, tubing diameter and length were adjusted in order to obtain temporal profile peaks as sharp and intense as possible (see scheme in Fig. S1).

The system consisted of a peristaltic pump, a narrow tube; an injection valve; and a micro reactor (mixing coil) in which nanoparticles and sample mix and interacts forming the product (CQDs-TU-Hg2+) that is sensed by a flow-through detection cell and recorded. Teflon tubes (0.8 mm i.d) were used to maintain identical flow rates in each feedline. A quartz flow-through cell (Hellma, Germany) with 80 µL volume and 10 mm optical path length was employed as a photoluminescence detection cell.

The analytical scheme consisted in pumping the probe dispersion, using the peristaltic pump (P), loading the sample loop (SL). A bypass loop allows passage of the carrier solution (in this case the analyte standard solution or sample solution) when the injection valve is in the load position. Both carrier solution (solution containing Hg2+) and working dispersion (CQD-TU probe dispersion) are constantly pumped to a waste container (W) until the injection valve is in injection position. As the valve is switched to injection position, the probe dispersion is introduced (326 μL) into the carrier stream forming a zone that enters to the reaction coil for better mixing of analyte with nanoparticles (D).

Studies using batch analysis were made by measuring photoluminescence after the original dialyzed working CQDs dispersions was diluted in water (1% v/v). These dispersions were transferred to quartz cuvettes to measure photoluminescence: CQDs at 370/460 nm, CQDs-GSH at 350/430 nm, CQDs-TU at 350/435 nm, or CQDs-TA at 328/414 nm. Photoluminescence (L) measured from CQDs working dispersions in the presence of Hg2+ were normalized by the signal measured from working dispersions in absence of Hg2+ (L0), in order to establish a mathematical relationship between normalized signal (L0/L) and Hg2+ concentration, C(Hg2+), where sensitivity is the quenching constant (Ks) as seen in Eq. (1).
(1)
L 0
/
L
= 1 +
K s
C
( H
g
2 +
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-degradation procedure of thiomersal to Hg2+ in vaccines samples
ce:section.ce:para: Hepatitis B and diphtheria−tetanus samples vaccines both with expected thiomersal concentration up to 100 μg mL−1 [15,16] were analyzed. Photo-degradation studies of thiomersal to Hg2+ were previously made and reported [17], but minor adjustments were made in the present work: a volume of 3 mL of diluted sample (140 times) was mixed with 100 µL of H2O2 and the volume was made up to10 mL with ultrapure water. These solutions were UV irradiated for 15 min in quartz tubes. Once the UV/H2O2 oxidation process was carried out, the sample solution was used as carrier flow in FIA ​ for the determination of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative results using an analytical method based on photo-degradation CV-AAS
ce:section.ce:para: Comparative analyzes were made by CV-AAS after thiomersal (along with any ethylmercury residue) was oxidixed into Hg2+, by UV exposure in the presence of H2O2 and CQDs (as photo-catalysts) and then measuring the Hg0 after further chemical reduction of Hg2+ using SnCl2 [10] as follows: Vaccine sample solutions (1 mL) were placed in glass test tubes, with final volume (10 mL) adjusted using H2O2 solution (at 1.0% v/v with pH previously adjusted to 4.5), also adding 17 μL of a CQD dispersion (made with only citric acid) and then water. Subsequently, the glass test tubes were placed on a circular rack inside the photo-reactor and kept rotating to be uniformly exposed to visible radiation (20 min) inside the photo-chemical reactor. After irradiation, solution was transferred to a glass reaction cell containing 2 mL of SnCl2 (20% v/v) solution in order to reduce Hg2+ to Hg0, which was transferred, by an airflow passing through the solution, to the CV-AAS system operating in continuous acquisition mode. All statistical comparison of results was made using Microsoft Excell, based on two-tail Student t-test with previous evaluation to determine similarity or not in variances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary studies of the interaction between Hg2+ and carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Since the proposed method is based on the photo-degradation of thiomersal, to obtain Hg2+ that is quantified through the photoluminescence changing of CQDs, preliminary studies were carried out to find the nanomaterial that provides a more efficient interaction with Hg2+ with photoluminescence suppression. Interaction studies using a dispersion of CQDs modified with compounds containing sulfur and nitrogen, that is, glutathione (CSH), thiourea (TU), and thioacetamide (TA) were performed. The different CQDs and their interaction with Hg2+ were evaluated in terms of photoluminescence variation aiming to select the photoluminescence analytical probe.

Photoluminescence from these CQDs-modified dispersions was measured before and after the addition of an aliquot of a Hg2+ stock solution, at the characteristic pH (3.9 4.1) of the diluted original dispersions. CQDs prepared only with citric acid (without doping with S and N) was used as a reference. It was observed that the addition of Hg2+ (at 50 μg L−1) produces luminescence quenching for all of the different CQDs and no spectral shifting was observed along with signal decreasing (relative quenching factors were calculated as L0/L). The least effective interaction with Hg2+, in terms of signal quenching, was observed for the CQDs made with only citric acid (14% signal decreasing). For CQD modified with sulfur and nitrogen, quenching (percent signal decreasing in parenthesis) were also found: CQDs-TU (25%), CQDs-TA (20%) and CQDs-GSH (17%).

Comparing the functionalized CQDs, modifiers that contains a thiol group, –HS, produced a less effective functionalization to enable interaction with Hg2+, when compared to functionalization achieved by using precursors containing –C=S, where sulfur is probably more exposed to interact with Hg2+ [18,19]. It is worth noticing that CQDs without functionalization produced the lowest signal quenching factor, demonstrating the importance of sulfur functionalization of CQDs to achieve interaction with mercury species.

As the concentration of CQDs in these dispersions cannot be directly obtained, their total carbon content was measured, in order to establish a reference values for the content of carbon present in the diluted aqueous dispersions that were used to detect Hg2+. Total carbon in the original dispersions (obtained right after dialysis) varied from 61 μg g−1 to 127 μg g−1 as seen in Table 2 . Total carbon of the working dispersions was function of the dilution factor that was adjusted taking into consideration the measured probe luminescence (about 900 arbitrary units) set for the quenching experiment.

The photoluminescence quenching effect produced by Hg2+ was monitored (Fig. 1 ) over time (right after addition of Hg2+, at 50 µg L−1, up to 40 min). Photoluminescence from CQDs-GSH continuously decrease along the first 10 min before reaching stability. In contrast, photoluminescence from CQDs-TA and CQDs-TU dispersions were promptly quenched, with intensities remaining stable during the period of the study. For CQDs prepared without S, N-containing modifiers, the interaction with Hg2+ decrease photoluminescence that did not reach stabilization over the 40 min experiment. Results indicated that CQDs-TU showed the best potential to be used as a probe for the quantification of Hg2+.

As CQDs-TU was chosen as the photoluminescent probe for quantification of Hg2+, an image was obtained using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), operating in the transmission mode. The morphology of the GQDs-TU is shown in Fig. 2 A (image analyzed after processing with Image-J software). Most nanoparticles were within 25 and 40 nm range although nanoparticles down to 10 nm and up to 70 nm were found (insert of Fig. 2A). The aspect ratio histogram (Fig. 2B) showed most nanoparticles presenting the ratio between the shortest and the longest axis within 1.1 and 1.3. Results also showed that most nanoparticles tend to be circular (circularity between 0.75 and 0.95) as seen in Fig. 2C.

The photoluminescence of CQDs-TU dispersions as a function of pH (2.0–9.0) were evaluated (without addition of Hg2+). The final pH values were adjusted with aliquots of HCl or NaOH solutions (at 0.01 mol L−1). As pH of the dispersion increased, photoluminescence became more intense (Fig. 3 A) with a blue spectral shifting (of about 20 nm) as depicted in Fig. 3B. This is most noticeable when the pH is changed from a more acidic to a less acidic condition (above pH 4.0).

Within the 2.0–4.0 pH range, only a small increase (around 18%) in photoluminescence intensity was noticed. However, a strong improvement was observed as the pH varied from 5 to 8 and then stabilizing at pH 9.

The ξ potentials of CQDs-TU were studied as a function of pH. The nanoparticle surface negative charge increases as the pH of the dispersion becomes higher (see ζ-potential values ​​in Fig. 3C), which indicates a lower degree of nanomaterial aggregation as the pH increased. This occurs probably due to the presence of -COOH groups, at the edges of the CQDs structures, that by losing protons becomes negatively charged. Such aggregation decreases quantum confinement, affecting photoluminescence as the conduction band – valence band (CB-VB) electron recombination is hindered [19].

Although pH studies apparently indicated that a less acidic medium would favor the sensitivity of the analytical method in terms of photoluminescence intensity, dispersion with pH 3.0 was chosen. When mixing Hg2+ with less acidic CQDs dispersion promotes formation of Hg(OH)2(aq) as well as Hg(OH)n n+2 (aq) species that may deposit onto surface of carbon nanoparticles (acting as nucleation sites) [20]. In this way, a layer of Hg(OH)2(s) passivates the surface of the GQDs, promptly extinguishing photoluminescence.

The nature of the interaction between the CQDs-TU and Hg2+ was studied by evaluating luminescence quenching at increasing concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0 to 30 μg L−1) under different temperatures (20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, and 35 °C) as seen in Fig. 4 . The photoluminescence quenching was more effective at lower temperatures with sensitivity about four times higher, at 20 °C, than the one obtained at 35 °C (Fig. 4). This effect is characteristic of static quenching as stability of long-term interaction between the nanoparticles and the analyte tend to decrease as temperature increases. This is corroborated by the Stern-Volmer constant (obtained by the sensitivities of the analytical curves obtained at different temperatures), which decreases almost 4 times from 20 °C to 35 °C.

The result indicates that CQDs-TU-Hg2+ species are formed but not necessarily with a defined stoichiometry, since a nanoparticle may present several sites to interact with Hg2+. Photoluminescence quenching is probably due to the withdrawal of electrons from the conduction band, making them unavailable for excitonic recombination. However, it is also possible that external heavy atom effect occurs, relaxing excited states involved. Photoluminescence time decay measurements were not made but it is valid to point out that typical lifetime of similar CQDs (CQDs-GSH) is about 12 ns and tend to not be modified by interacting with metal ions [14].

The formation of a non-luminescent CQDs-TU-Hg2+species agrees with the change of net surface charge of nanoparticle towards positive values (less negative surface charge) as Hg2+ is added to the system. At pH 4, ξ-potential of –(26.0 ± 0.6) mV was measured and when Hg2+ was added, ξ-potential changed to –(14.9 ± 1.7) mV. At pH 7.0, ξ-potentials observed before and after the addition of Hg2+were respectively –(37.5 ± 0.4) mV and –(21.8 ± 2.4) mV. Results indicated that the use of the CQDs-TU as a probe can be made at a pH that corresponds to the original pH of the dispersion after dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method to flow injection analysis

ce:section.ce:para: In order to use the CQDs-TU dispersion as the analytical probe FIA, conditions were adjusted to provide fiagrams with intense and sharp peaks. The 2.5 mL min−1 flow rate, was used as it enabled sharp peak time profiles and good profile between sequential injections (made at 15 s intervals). Higher flow rates, for instance at 5.0 mL min−1, produced peak profiles that did not to reach baseline before subsequent injection.

Sample volume (Sv) was selected in function of the length of the available sampling loops (5 cm loop, providing injection of 25 μL, and 60 cm loop, providing injection of 325 μL). Injection of 25 μL produced small peaks (degrading sensitivity of determination) while the use of 325 μL produced good analytical response with intense and sharp peaks. Longer sampling loops were not tested, as larger sampled volumes tend to produce larger analytical zones and increased carryover effects.

Along flow rate, tubbing length influences the analytical response. It provides enough time for mixing of reactants and time for reaction to occur (not necessarily until its completion). Two different reaction coil lengths were compared with the shorter coil (30 cm) did not provide proper interaction of reactants, resulting in significant variation in measured signal. The use of the longer mixing coil (200 cm), along with a flow rate of 2.5 mL min−1, produced small signal variation and kept a sharp peak profile, also providing larger analytical frequency (at least 3 injections min−1) with 15 s interval between injections. In this way, the analytical frequency is 90 measurements h−1, considering 1 min washing flush with carrier after triplicate measurements. As the spectrofluorometer was used as detection system, the setup detection conditions were chosen as indicated in Table 1 with adequate detection duty cycle for the analysis achieved with integration time of 0.2 s.

Under the conditions chosen for FIA, different dilutions of the dialyzed dispersions were used to obtain the ones to be introduced into the FIA system, forming the detected zones. Three different dilution factors were tested (10; 20 and 100 times) and the resulted reproducible peaks in terms of peak intensity with proper resolution between them under the injection interval of 15 s. The dilution factor of 100 times was chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures of merit in FIA
ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal was indirectly determined by measuring the Hg2+ formed after photochemical degradation, as reported in the literature [10], as exposition to UV, in the presence of H2O2, favors rapid degradation of thiomersal into Hg2+. In this sense, analytical curves were prepared by using Hg2+ standard solutions.

Analytical information by FIA was obtained by first measuring photoluminescence from the introduced volume of CQD-TU probe into water used as carrier flow (blank signal). Then, the water was changed by each of the Hg2+ standard solutions, as carriers, to obtain the photoluminescence quenched signals used to construct the analytical curve by creating zones of introduced CQDs-TU dispersion. Between each analyte concentration, the carrier was allowed to flow for 1 min, in order to properly flush the previous standard solution, before introduction of probe (four sequential injections for analyte standard). The analyte standard solutions, used as carriers, presented Hg2+ concentrations varied from 25 to 900 μg L−1 (fiagram shown in Fig. 5 A).

The normalized analytical curve (Fig. 5B) presented a linear behavior (R2 = 0.997) covering the concentration range from 25 (instrumental LOQ) to 900 µg L−1 (final concentration). The equation model for the calibration curve was (L0/L) = (1.32 ± 0.02) × 10−3 C Hg2+ (L μg−1) + (1.10 ± 0.01). Instrumental limit of quantification (LOQ) was the lower concentration in this range that still kept linearity of response (at least at R2 = 0.997). If keeping 100 fold dilution after photochemical degradation, the sample limit of quantification in 2.5 mg L−1 (or 2.5 μg mL−1), which is far lower that typical concentrations of thiomersal in vaccines.

Instrumental precision was obtained as the variation coefficient of 10 consecutive injection producing signal measurements at the analyte concentrations at both 25 and 100 μg L−1. The precisions were respectively 2.0% and 1.8%. Accuracy of the method was evaluated by determining two different concentration levels of Hg2+ in ultrapure water (at 25 and at 100 μg L−1). The recoveries obtained (n = 4) were respectively 95.0 ± 3.2% and 91.3 ± 3.8%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of possible interferences in determining thiomersal in vaccines
ce:section.ce:para: During the photo-degradation process of thiomersal, different degradation products can be generated, such as thiosalicylic acid, and dithiobenzoic acid, therefore, studies were carried out to evaluate the possible interference that these products could impose on the photoluminescence of the CQDs-TU probe and in its interaction with Hg2+.

Interference was evaluated by the ratio between the signal measured from a solution containing only the analyte and the one measured from a solution containing analyte and a potential interferent (analyte concentration was kept the same in both solutions at 200 µg L−1).

If the Ianalyte/I(analyte+potential interferent) values were close to unity, no interference occurs. Values of Ianalyte/I(analyte+potential interferent) larger than 1 would indicate further quenching of the probe due to the contribution of the component generated during photo-degradation process. These tests were carried out with three analyte:interferent concentration ratios (1:1, 1:2 and 1:4). Measurements were performed in triplicate.

Thiosalicylic acid and dithiobenzoic acid did not impose any interference. Other components present in vaccine samples also were evaluated, such as, Al3+, Na+, K+, chloride, dihydrogen phosphate, in the range of 100–500 µg L−1. But it was found that these compounds did not affect Hg2+ signal probably due to the large dilution factor (140 times) used. Fiagram at analyte:concomitant species proportion at 1:1 are shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thiomersal using the CQDs-Tu probe in flow regime after off-line photo-degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The quantification of thiomersal in hepatitis B and diphtheria-tetanus vaccines in multi-dose vials was made with the proposed method. Thiomersal concentration in both vaccines is 100 μg mL−1, therefore, the samples were diluted with water. Subsequently, 3 mL aliquots of these solutions were placed in glass tubes containing 100 μL of H2O2 and then further diluted with water (10 mL). These sample solutions were subjected to UV irradiation for 15 min in the same glass tubes. Once the UV/H2O2 oxidation process was completed, the sample solution was transferred to the FIA system for Hg2+ determination.

The thiomersal concentrations were (94.5 ± 1.2)% (n = 3) for the hepatitis B and (93.8 ± 3.8)% (n = 3) diphtheria-tetanus vaccines. Samples were also analyzed (n = 3) by cold vapor-AAS using the method reported by Miranda-Andrades et al. [10]. Percent recoveries were (95.7 ± 3.2)% in hepatitis B vaccine and (94.8 ± 4.3)% in diphtheria-tetanus vaccine. Student t-test for similar variances (confidence limit of 95%; two tails, each with α/2 = 0.025) indicated statistical agreement of results (texperimental = 0.70 < tcritical = 4.3 for hepatitis B vaccine and texperimental = 0.34 < tcritical = 4.3 for tetanus-diphteria B vaccine). Based on these results, it can be concluded that the proposed method was adequate for a simple, selective and sensitive determination of thiomersal in vaccines samples.

To assess the greenness of the proposed analytical procedures, an easy-to-use approach, AGREE, developed as software, was used. AGREE has been recommended for the general evaluation of the greenness of analytical methods [21] in which reagent toxicity, waste generated, power requirements, miniaturization, and automation are some of the criteria to assess the ecological character of an analytical methodology. The evaluation criteria took into consideration the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry setting the final combination of scores within a scale from 0 to 1, presenting the result visually as a colored pictogram with the overall score of the method in the center and with each segment (each one for one of the principles) displaying a color tending to the darker green as greener is the procedure. The final score of the proposed method is 0.76 (see Fig. 6 for the pictogram).

The results showed that the proposed method has obvious advantages in most principles (4, 6, 9, 10 and 11). Principle 4 advocates that the number of analytical steps should be kept to a minimum, and there is no application of derivatization agents (principle 6). Regarding the evaluation of the energy consumed (principle 9) in the sample preparation, analytical sampling, reactant mixing, and detection steps, requires only the electrical energy to run the spectrofluorimeter, FIA pump, computers and UV lamps for thiomersal photo-degradation process. In addition, the use of CQDs inserts an additional value to the proposed method as it is a carbon-based and nontoxic nanomaterial that replace the use of toxic and expensive fluorogenic agents (principle 10 and 11).

Principle 8 considers sample throughput and takes the number of samples that can be processed within 1 h and the proposed method easily processes 30 samples/h. Principle 5 favors automation and miniaturization and as thiomersal photo-oxidation is carried out off-line (principle 1), it presented a low score. However, the developed method is based on an automated flow injection system and a photo-degradation could be carried out in a flow system operating to deliver the carrier of analyte used as the carrier flow in the quantification flow system. It is worth noting that despite generating toxic residues due to the nature of the detected species (inorganic mercury), the amount of sample required for each sampling is small (principle 2) and the concentration is at trace level (µg L−1) therefore, the waste is generated in minimum quantities (principle 7).

The proposed method for the quantitation of thiomersal in vaccines is simple, with high analtical frequency and it is environment friendly. The FIA system reduces the amount of reagents used, therefore the waste produced. In addition, nontoxic nanomaterials (CQDs) for the environment and human health were used, giving a significant contribution toward the implementation of green analytical chemistry. For vaccine samples, dilution was the only sample treatment required, before photo-degradation. Results are in agreement with those obtained using a cold vapor-AAS with a recovery ranging between 93 and 94% and enabled proper limit of quantification for the proposed intent.
Nothing to declare.

Claudiomar R. Franco: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jarol R. Miranda-Andrades: Methodology, Formal analysis. Carlos A.T. Toloza: Formal analysis. Dunieskys G. Larrudé: Formal analysis. Luis Maqueira-Espinosa: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ricardo Q. Aucelio: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Anna De Falco: Formal analysis. Marlin J. Pedrozo-Peñafiel: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

Aucelio thanks scholarships from Brazilian agencies FAPERJ (E-26/200.810/2021) and CNPq (306108/2021-6) and scientific grants from FAPERJ (SEI 260003/001187/2020 Ref 210.104/2020 and SEI 260003/01131/2021 Ref 210.726/2021) and CNPq (409090/2018-2). Authors are also thankful for analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2023.100184.
